name: Shuten-doji Slayer
description: The Shuten-doji Slayer embodies courage, cunning, and unwavering resolve in the face of overwhelming evil. This hero, often associated with Minamoto no Yorimitsu (also known as Raiko), is celebrated for leading a daring expedition to Mount Ōe to vanquish Shuten-doji, a monstrous oni (demon) chieftain who terrorized the region surrounding Kyoto. Shuten-doji, infamous for his insatiable appetite for human flesh and his hedonistic lifestyle within his mountain fortress, presented a formidable threat to the peace and security of the land. The Slayer, accompanied by his loyal and skilled retainers, disguised themselves as yamabushi (mountain ascetics) to gain entry into Shuten-doji's domain. Through a combination of strategy, bravery, and the use of enchanted sake that lulled the demon and his followers into a drunken stupor, the Slayer and his companions were able to strike a decisive blow, ultimately decapitating Shuten-doji and liberating the captured villagers. This legendary feat solidified the Slayer's reputation as a national hero, a symbol of justice, and a protector of the innocent. The tale of the Shuten-doji Slayer is a popular motif in Japanese folklore, literature, and theater, serving as a timeless narrative about the triumph of good over evil and the importance of unwavering dedication to the greater good. The Slayer's legacy continues to inspire generations with its depiction of bravery, strategic brilliance, and the unwavering pursuit of justice. The identity of the Slayer may vary across different versions of the tale, but the core narrative remains consistent: a courageous figure who risks everything to defeat a powerful and malevolent oni.
subtitle: Vanquisher of the Oni King

keyMyths.title: The Oni of Mount Ōe
keyMyths.description: Shuten-doji, a fearsome oni, resided on Mount Ōe, terrorizing the surrounding villages and consuming human flesh. His reign of terror prompted the emperor to send a brave hero to vanquish him.

keyMyths.title: The Disguise
keyMyths.description: The Slayer and his companions disguised themselves as yamabushi (mountain ascetics) to deceive Shuten-doji and gain access to his fortress. This allowed them to study the layout and plan their attack.

keyMyths.title: The Enchanted Sake
keyMyths.description: The Slayer offered Shuten-doji and his oni followers enchanted sake, which lulled them into a drunken stupor. This provided the opportunity to strike a decisive blow and defeat the demon king.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: A collection of Japanese fairy tales, including a version of the Shuten-doji tale.
sources.text: A collection of Japanese fairy tales, including a version of the Shuten-doji tale.

sources.author: Anonymous

sources.text: A collection of over one thousand tales from India, China, and Japan, including a version of the Shuten-doji legend.

associations.name: Courage
associations.significance: Facing overwhelming odds against a powerful demon.

associations.name: Sword
associations.significance: Symbolizes the hero's strength and ability to vanquish evil.

associations.name: Cunning
associations.significance: The hero used strategy and disguise to infiltrate Shuten-doji's lair.

associations.name: Poisoned Sake
associations.significance: Used to weaken Shuten-doji, representing the use of intellect over brute force.

associations.name: Mount Ōe
associations.significance: The demonic mountain stronghold, representing the heart of evil.

associations.name: Loyalty
associations.significance: Represented by the hero's unwavering service to the Emperor and the people.

associations.name: Leadership
associations.significance: Demonstrates the ability to command and inspire others to fight for justice.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both heroes undertake dangerous quests to defeat monstrous foes and protect humanity.
Both are powerful warriors who battle monstrous beings to safeguard their realms.
Both are celebrated for slaying a monstrous beast that threatened the populace.

worshipPractices.name: Festivals
worshipPractices.description: Local festivals are held in regions associated with the Shuten-doji legend, celebrating the hero's victory and ensuring continued protection from evil.

worshipPractices.name: Shrines
worshipPractices.description: Some shrines are dedicated or include elements commemorating Minamoto no Yorimitsu and his victory, venerating him as a protector.

cultural.modernLegacy: The tale of the Shuten-doji Slayer continues to inspire modern adaptations in literature, film, manga, and video games, solidifying the hero's place as a cultural icon representing courage and the triumph of good over evil. The story is often reinterpreted with contemporary themes, showcasing its enduring relevance.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: While often attributed to Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raiko), the early life of the Shuten-doji Slayer is shrouded in legend. Yorimitsu was a prominent figure in the Heian period, known for his military prowess and loyalty to the Imperial Court. Born into a powerful clan, he received extensive training in martial arts and strategy, preparing him for a life of service and leadership. The historical Yorimitsu was known for his suppression of bandits and other threats to the peace of the capital. The legend of the Shuten-doji Slayer likely grew out of these real-life exploits, transforming him into a mythical hero capable of confronting supernatural forces. The blending of historical fact and folklore created a figure who embodies both earthly power and divine favor, making him a compelling symbol of justice and protection.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests
extendedContent.content: The Shuten-doji Slayer's most famous quest is undoubtedly the subjugation of Shuten-doji, a formidable oni who resided on Mount Ōe. According to the tale, Shuten-doji and his demonic horde terrorized the region, kidnapping women and pillaging villages. To confront this evil, Minamoto no Yorimitsu, along with his loyal retainers, journeyed to Mount Ōe disguised as mountain ascetics. They gained Shuten-doji's trust and offered him poisoned sake. As the demon chieftain and his followers succumbed to the effects of the poison, Yorimitsu and his men launched their attack, ultimately slaying Shuten-doji and liberating the region from his reign of terror. This victory solidified Yorimitsu's reputation as a legendary hero and a champion of the people, ensuring his place in Japanese folklore. The quest represents the triumph of courage, cunning, and unwavering resolve against seemingly insurmountable odds.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: The legacy of the Shuten-doji Slayer extends far beyond the historical figure of Minamoto no Yorimitsu, permeating Japanese culture and inspiring countless artistic representations. He is celebrated as a symbol of bravery, justice, and the power of good to overcome evil. The story of his triumph over Shuten-doji continues to be retold in various forms, including Noh plays, Kabuki performances, and contemporary media such as manga, anime, and video games. The Shuten-doji Slayer's image is often invoked in festivals and rituals aimed at warding off evil spirits and ensuring prosperity. His legacy serves as a reminder of the importance of courage, loyalty, and the unwavering pursuit of justice in the face of adversity. The enduring popularity of the Shuten-doji Slayer demonstrates the timeless appeal of the hero's journey and its ability to resonate across generations.

shortDescription: A courageous hero, often identified as Minamoto no Yorimitsu, renowned for slaying the oni Shuten-doji and liberating Kyoto from his terror.
